subject: Mathematics
q: The area enclosed by the curves $y=x^2, y=x^3$, $x=0$ and $x=p$, where $p>1$, is $1 / 6$. The $\mathrm{p}$ equals
question: The area enclosed by the curves $y=x^2, y=x^3$, $x=0$ and $x=p$, where $p>1$, is $1 / 6$. The $\mathrm{p}$ equals
solution:
Given curves are $y=x^2$ and $y=x^3$ Also, $x=0$ and $x=p, p>1$ Now, intersecting point is $(1,1)$
Required Area $$ \begin{aligned} & =\int_0^1\left(x^2-x^3 d x+\int_1^p x^3\right)-x^2 d x \\ & \frac{1}{6}=\frac{x^3}{3}-\left.\frac{x^4}{4}\right|_0 ^1+\frac{x^4}{4}-\left.\frac{x^3}{3}\right|_1 ^p \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{6}=\left(\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{4}\right)+\left(\frac{p^4}{4}-\frac{p^3}{3}-\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{3}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{6}-\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{3}=\frac{3 p^4-4 p^3}{12} \\ & \left.\Rightarrow \frac{p^3(3 p-4}{12}\right)=0 \Rightarrow p^3(3 p-4)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow p=0 \text { or } \frac{4}{3} \\ & \text { Since, it is given that } p>1 \\ & \therefore p \text { can not be zero. } \\ & \text { Hence, } p=\frac{4}{3} \end{aligned} $$

q: The area of the region <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>≤</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>24</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is______.
question: The area of the region <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>≤</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>24</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is______.
solution:
We have,

S=(x,y):3x2≤4y≤6x+24

Then,

y=3x24

4y=6x+24

Solving both equations, we get -2,3 and 4,12.

Required area

A=∫−24 6x+244−3x24dx

⇒A=14∫−24 6x+24−3x2dx

⇒A=34x2+6x−x34−24

⇒A=12+24−16-3+12-2

⇒A=27 sq.units

q:
Consider a curve y=yx in the first quadrant as shown in the figure. Let the area A1 is twice the area A2. Then the normal to the curve perpendicular to the line 2x-12y=15 does NOT pass through the point __

​​​​​​​
question:
Consider a curve y=yx in the first quadrant as shown in the figure. Let the area A1 is twice the area A2. Then the normal to the curve perpendicular to the line 2x-12y=15 does NOT pass through the point __

​​​​​​​
solution:
Given that A1=2A2

Now, from the graph

A1+A2=x×y-2×4=xy-8

⇒32 A1=xy-8

⇒A1=23xy-163

⇒∫4xfxdx=23xy-163

⇒fx=23xdydx+y

⇒23xdydx=y3

⇒2∫dyy=∫dxx

⇒2lny=lnx+lnc

⇒y2=cx 

As f4=2⇒c=1

So y2=x is the given curve.

Given that the slope of normal =-6

So the equation of normal will be 

y=-6x-12-6-14-63

⇒y=-6x+3+54

⇒y+6x=57

Only 10,-4 does not satisfy from the given options.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the area of the region bounded by the curves \(y=4-\frac{x^2}{4}\) and \(y=\frac{x-4}{2}\) is equal to \(\alpha\), then \(6 \alpha\) equals
question: If the area of the region bounded by the curves \(y=4-\frac{x^2}{4}\) and \(y=\frac{x-4}{2}\) is equal to \(\alpha\), then \(6 \alpha\) equals
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Area }=\int_{-6}^4\left\{\left(4-\frac{x^2}{4}\right)-\left(\frac{x-4}{2}\right)\right\} d x \\ & =\int_{-6}^4\left\{-\frac{x^2}{4}-\frac{x-6}{2}\right\} d x \\ & \alpha=-\frac{x^3}{12}-\frac{x^2}{4}+\left.6 x\right|_{-6} ^4=\frac{125}{3} \\ & \therefore 6 \alpha=250\end{aligned}$

solution:
Given: y2≤4x, x<4, xyx-1x-2x-3x-4>0

Case - I: y>0

⇒xx-1x-2x-3x-4>0

⇒x∈0, 1∪2, 3   ...i

Case – II : y<0

xx-1x-2x-3x-4<0

⇒x∈1, 2∪3, 4   ...ii

So, the required area is given by,

A=∫044xdx {Take the mirror image of strips in the first quadrant to find the area}

⇒A=2∫04xdx

⇒A=2·23x3204

⇒A=2·23432

⇒A=2×23×8

⇒A=323

solution:
R=x,y:max0,logex≤y≤2x,12≤x≤2

So, the area of the region is

∫1222xdx-∫12ℓnxdx

=2xln21/22-xlnx-x12

=22-21/2loge2-2ln2-1

=22-2loge2-2ln2+1

∴ α=22-2,β=-2,γ=1

So, α+β-2γ2

=22-2-2-22

=-22=2

.f'(x)=6x2-6x-12=6x2-x-2

=6(x-2)(x+1)

Hence, x=-1 is the point of local maximum.

Also, f'2-<0 & f'2+>0

Hence, x=2 is the point of local minimum.

∴a=-1 & b=2

Points=2,-20 & -1,7

=x42-x3-6x2-10-x42-x3-6x202

=92+24=572⇒4 A=114

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $f(\alpha)$ denote the area of the region in the first quadrant bounded by $x=0, x=1, y^{2}=x$ and $y=|\alpha x-5|-|1-\alpha x|+\alpha x^{2}$. Then $(f(0)+f(1))$ is equal to
question: Let $f(\alpha)$ denote the area of the region in the first quadrant bounded by $x=0, x=1, y^{2}=x$ and $y=|\alpha x-5|-|1-\alpha x|+\alpha x^{2}$. Then $(f(0)+f(1))$ is equal to
solution:
For $\alpha = 0$: curve is $y = 5 - 1 = 4$ (horizontal line), area $= \int_0^1 (4 - \sqrt{x}) dx = 4 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{10}{3}$
For $\alpha = 1$ and $0 \leq x \leq 1$: $|x-5| = 5-x$, $|1-x| = 1-x$, so $y = 4 + x^2$
Area $= \int_0^1 (4 + x^2 - \sqrt{x}) dx = 4 + \frac{1}{3} - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{11}{3}$
$f(0) + f(1) = \frac{10}{3} + \frac{11}{3} = 7$

solution:
Plotting the diagram of x2+y2≤21 & y2≤4x for x≥1 we get,

Now from above diagram required Area Δ=2∫132xdx+2∫32121-x2dx

⇒Δ=8333-1+21sin-127-63

So, 12Δ-21sin-127

=128333-1+21sin-127-63-21sin-127

=128333-1-63

=23-832

=3-43

question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mo>[</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>]</mo><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi></math> be given as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="" open="{"><mrow><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>0</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>m</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>x</mi><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable><mo>.</mo></mrow></mfenced></math>If the area bounded by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> sq units, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mi>A</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
Given f:[-3,1]→R, fx=min(x+6), x2,-3≤x≤0maxx, x2, 0≤x≤1

We have x+6=x2

⇒x2-x-6=0

⇒x-3x+2=0

⇒x=3 & -2.

Also, x=x2

⇒x2-x=0

⇒xx32-1=0

⇒x=0 & x=1.

Hence, the region bounded by fx is given by the following graph.

Hence, the area bounded by y=f(x) and x-axis is

A=∫-3-2(x+6)dx+∫-20x2dx+∫01xdx

⇒A=x22+6x-3-2+x33-20+2x32301

⇒A=42-12-92-18+0+83+2×1-03

⇒A=-10+272+83+23

⇒A=416 sq units.

⇒6A=41.

q: The area (in sq. units) of the region $\{x \in R: x \geq 0, y \geq 0, y \geq x-2$ and $y \leq \sqrt{x}\}$, is
question: The area (in sq. units) of the region $\{x \in R: x \geq 0, y \geq 0, y \geq x-2$ and $y \leq \sqrt{x}\}$, is
solution: The intersection point of $y=x-2$ and $y=\sqrt{x}$ is $(4,2)$. The required area $$ =\int_0^4 \sqrt{x} d x-\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 2=\frac{16}{3}-2=\frac{10}{3} $$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the area of the region $\left\{(x, y): 1-2 x \leqslant y \leqslant 4-x^{2}, x \geqslant 0, y \geqslant 0\right\}$ is $\frac{\alpha}{\beta}, \alpha, \beta \in \mathbf{N}, \operatorname{gcd}(\alpha, \beta)=1$, then the value of $(\alpha+\beta)$ is :
question: If the area of the region $\left\{(x, y): 1-2 x \leqslant y \leqslant 4-x^{2}, x \geqslant 0, y \geqslant 0\right\}$ is $\frac{\alpha}{\beta}, \alpha, \beta \in \mathbf{N}, \operatorname{gcd}(\alpha, \beta)=1$, then the value of $(\alpha+\beta)$ is :
solution:
Required area $= \dfrac{2}{3} \times 8 - \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1$
$= \dfrac{16}{3} - \dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{61}{12} = \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha + \beta = 73$

question: The area (in sq. units) of the region, given by the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>×</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>∣</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>≤</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is :
solution:
Given curves are y=2x2 & y=4-2x

So, 2x2=4-2x

⇒2x2+2x-4=0⇒x-1x+2=0

So, the x-coordinate of the intersection point is 1 as x=-2<0

Required area ∫014-2x-2x2dx=4x-x2-2x3301

=4-1-23=73

subject: Mathematics
q: The area of the region $\mathrm{R}=\left\{(x, y): x y \leq 8,1 \leq y \leq x^{2}, x \geq 0\right\}$ is
question: The area of the region $\mathrm{R}=\left\{(x, y): x y \leq 8,1 \leq y \leq x^{2}, x \geq 0\right\}$ is
solution:
The region $R$ is defined by $xy \le 8$, $1 \le y \le x^2$, and $x \ge 0$.
First, find the intersection points of the curves.
Intersection of $y = 1$ and $y = x^2$: $x^2 = 1 \implies x = 1$ (since $x \ge 0$).
Intersection of $y = x^2$ and $xy = 8$: $x(x^2) = 8 \implies x^3 = 8 \implies x = 2$. At $x=2$, $y=4$.
Intersection of $xy = 8$ and $y = 1$: $x(1) = 8 \implies x = 8$.
The region is bounded below by $y = 1$ from $x = 1$ to $x = 8$.
The upper boundary consists of two parts:
From $x = 1$ to $x = 2$, the upper curve is $y = x^2$.
From $x = 2$ to $x = 8$, the upper curve is $y = \frac{8}{x}$.
The area $A$ is given by:
$A = \int_{1}^{2} (x^2 - 1) dx + \int_{2}^{8} (\frac{8}{x} - 1) dx$
$A = [\frac{x^3}{3} - x]_1^2 + [8 \log_e x - x]_2^8$
$A = (\frac{8}{3} - 2) - (\frac{1}{3} - 1) + (8 \log_e 8 - 8) - (8 \log_e 2 - 2)$
$A = (\frac{2}{3}) - (-\frac{2}{3}) + 8(3 \log_e 2) - 8 - 8 \log_e 2 + 2$
$A = \frac{4}{3} + 24 \log_e 2 - 8 \log_e 2 - 6$
$A = 16 \log_e 2 + \frac{4}{3} - 6$
$A = 16 \log_e 2 - \frac{14}{3}$
$A = \frac{2}{3} (24 \log_e 2 - 7)$

q: The area (in sq. units) of the region <math><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{" separators="|"><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>×</mo><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is
question: The area (in sq. units) of the region <math><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{" separators="|"><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>×</mo><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is
solution:
The area of the region OCD is
=∫014-x2+3xdx=4x-x33-3x2201=4-13-32=136
So, the area of the given region OABC is
= Area of OABD - Area of OCD
=3×4-136
=596

q: If the area enclosed by the parabolas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is equal to the area enclosed by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> is equal to _____ .
question: If the area enclosed by the parabolas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is equal to the area enclosed by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> is equal to _____ .
solution:
Given:

P1:2y=5x2

P2:x2-y+6=0

On solving both equations, we have

2y=5y-6

⇒3y=30

⇒y=10

⇒x=±2

So, points of intersection are 2,10 & -2,10.

Also, solving P1 and y=αx, we have

x=0, 2α5

According to question 2∫02x2+6-5x22dx=∫02α5αx-5x22dx

⇒2x33+6x-5x3602=αx22-5x3602α5

⇒212+83-203=2α325-8α36×25

⇒2α375=16

⇒ α3=600

question: The area (in sq. units) of the region <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}" separators="|"><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
We have to find the area of the region A=x, y:y22≤x≤y+4

Now, to find the point of intersection of the curves x=y22 and x=y+4, we equate the value of x, to get

y22=y+4

⇒y2=2y+8

⇒y2-2y-8=0

⇒y-4y+2=0

⇒y=4 or y=-2

And, the graph of the given functions is as

The shaded area is the required area, and 

Area=∫-24xline-xparabolady

=∫-24y+4-y22dy

Now, using ∫xndx=xn+1n+1, we get

Area=y22+4y-y36-24

=8+16-323-2-8+43

=18 sq units.

q: If the area of the region $\left\{(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}): 1+\mathrm{x}^2 \leq \mathrm{y} \leq \min \{\mathrm{x}+7,11-3 \mathrm{x}\}\right\}$ is A , then 3 A is equal to
question: If the area of the region $\left\{(\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}): 1+\mathrm{x}^2 \leq \mathrm{y} \leq \min \{\mathrm{x}+7,11-3 \mathrm{x}\}\right\}$ is A , then 3 A is equal to
solution: $\begin{aligned} & A=\int_{-2}^1\left(x+7-x^2-1\right) d x+\int_1^2\left(11+3 x-x^2-1\right) d x \\ & =\left[\frac{x^2}{2}+6 x-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{-2}^1+\left[10 x-\frac{3 x^2}{2}-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_1^2 \\ & =\frac{50}{3} \Rightarrow 3 A=50 \quad \text { Option (1) }\end{aligned}$

question: The area of the region bounded by the curves $y=|x-2|, x=1, x=3$ and the $x$-axis is

q: The area of the region enclosed between the parabolas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></math> is.
question: The area of the region enclosed between the parabolas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></math> is.
solution:
We have, y2=2x-1   ...1 and y2=4x-3   ...2

We first find the points of intersection of the given parabolas by solving the equations 1 & 2simultaneously.

This gives, 2x-1=4 x-3

⇒  2x=2  ⇒x=1

Now, put x=1 in equation 1, we get y=±1.

Now, the equations 1 & 2 can be written as

y2=2x-12

y2=4x-34 respectively.

The graphs of these two curves are shown in figure.

We have to find the area of the shaded region.

Now, the equations 1 & 2 can be written as

x=y2+12 & x=y2+34 respectively.

It is clear from the figure, required area A=2∫01x2-x1dy  (∵ Area is symmetrical about x- axis)

where x1=y2+12 and  x2=y2+34

⇒A=2∫01y2+34-y2+12dy =24∫011-y2dy

=12y-y3301   =121-13

=1223=13 sq.units.

=29x-x303

subject: Mathematics
q: The area bounded by the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math> is
question: The area bounded by the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math> is
solution:
We first find the points of intersection of the curves y=x2-9 and y=3

which gives, x2-9=3  ⇒x2-9=±3

⇒x2=12 (or) x2=6 ⇒x=±12 (or) x=±6

The graphs of the two curves are shown in figure

We have to find the area of the shaded region.

Required area =2A1+2A2  (∵ The areas A1 & A2 are symmetrical about y- axis )

where A1=∫069-x2−3dx 

A1=∫066-x2dx=6x-x3306 =66-663=1263=46

A2=∫633−9−x2dx+∫3123−x2−9dx

=∫63-6+x2dx+∫31212-x2dx

=-6x+x3363+12x-x33312

=-18+9--66+26)+1212-412-36-9

=-36+46+812

Hence, required area =2A1+2A2

=246+2-36+46+812

=86-72+86+1612

=166+1612-72

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the area of the bounded region $\left\{(x, y): 0 \leq 9 x \leq y^2, y \geq 3 x-6\right\}$ be A. Then 6 A is equal to ________
question: Let the area of the bounded region $\left\{(x, y): 0 \leq 9 x \leq y^2, y \geq 3 x-6\right\}$ be A. Then 6 A is equal to ________
solution:
$0 \leq 9 x \leq y^2 \& y \geq 3 x-6$

$\begin{aligned} & A=\text { Required Area }=\left[\int_0^1(-3 \sqrt{x}) d x-\int_0^1(3 x-6) d x\right] \\ & A=-\left.3\left(\frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\frac{3}{2}}\right)\right|_0 ^1-\left.\left(\frac{3 x^2}{2}-6 x\right)\right|_0 ^1 \\ & A=-2[1-0]\left[\frac{3}{2}-6\right] \\ & A=-2-\frac{3}{2}+6=\frac{5}{2} \text { Sq. unit } \\ & \therefore 6 A=6 \times \frac{5}{2}=15\end{aligned}$

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> be the area bounded by the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and the ordinates <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi></math> is equal to _____ .
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> be the area bounded by the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and the ordinates <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi></math> is equal to _____ .
solution:
Plotting the y=xx-3 and x-axis between x=-1 and x=2 we get,

Now area of the above region is given by A=∫-103x-x2dx+∫023x-x2dx

⇒A=3×x22-x33-10+3×x22-x3302

⇒A=116+103=11+206=316

Hence, the value of 12A=62
